text: She has been working here ___ 2015.
explain: 'Since' introduces a point in time; 'for' takes a duration.

text: He is the best player ___ our team.
explain: We say 'the best in a group/team/class'.

text: She speaks English ___ than her brother.
explain: Comparative of the adverb 'well' is 'better'.

text: The children were ___ tired to finish their homework.
explain: 'Too + adjective + to + verb' expresses excess preventing an action.

text: We ___ lived in this city for three years.
explain: Present perfect with 'we' takes 'have'.

text: If you heat water to 100°C, it ___.
explain: Zero conditional: present simple in both clauses for general truths.

text: There isn't ___ milk left in the fridge.
explain: 'Much' goes with uncountable nouns in negatives.

text: The museum ___ every day except Mondays.
explain: Simple present for routines and schedules.

text: I haven't seen her ___ last week.
explain: 'Since' marks the starting point of a continuing period.

text: He ___ to the gym every morning before work.
explain: Third-person singular takes -s/-es in simple present.

text: She ___ dinner when I called her yesterday.
explain: Past continuous for an action in progress at a past moment.

text: There ___ a lot of traffic this morning.
explain: 'Traffic' is uncountable and singular; past form is 'was'.

text: If I ___ more time, I would travel the world.
explain: Second conditional: 'If' + past simple → 'would' + base form.

text: By the time we arrived, the movie ___ started.
explain: Past perfect for an action completed before another past action.

text: I'm not used to ___ up so early.
explain: 'Used to + -ing' means being accustomed to something.

text: I wish I ___ how to play the guitar.
explain: Wishes about present situations use the past simple.

text: The report ___ by the manager tomorrow.
explain: Future passive: will + be + past participle.

text: Neither my sister ___ I enjoy horror films.
explain: 'Neither...nor' is a correlative pair for negative coordination.

text: I look forward ___ you soon.
explain: 'Look forward to' is followed by a gerund.

text: This is the man ___ car was stolen.
explain: 'Whose' shows possession in relative clauses.

text: She asked me where ___.
explain: Indirect questions use normal word order and backshift the tense.

text: He denied ___ the vase.
explain: 'Deny' is followed by a gerund.

text: The solution ___ at the conference was widely adopted.
explain: Reduced passive relative clause: 'which was proposed' → 'proposed'.

text: You ___ have told me you were coming — I would have cooked!
explain: 'Should have + past participle' expresses past regret or obligation not fulfilled.

text: I'd rather you ___ smoke in the house.
explain: 'I'd rather' + subject + past simple expresses a preference about someone else.

text: Hardly ___ I sat down when the phone rang.
explain: Negative adverb inversion: 'Hardly + had + subject + past participle'.

text: Only then ___ the magnitude of the problem.
explain: 'Only + adverbial' at the front of a clause triggers subject-auxiliary inversion.

text: If I had taken the other job, I ___ in Paris now.
explain: Mixed conditional: past condition with present result uses 'would + be + -ing'.

text: She insisted that he ___ on time.
explain: After verbs of insistence, English uses the base (subjunctive) form.

text: ___ for her help, I wouldn't have finished on time.
explain: Inverted third conditional: 'Had + subject + (not) + past participle'.

text: The painting ___ to be worth a fortune.
explain: Reporting passive: 'subject + is said + to-infinitive'.

text: It is high time we ___ this issue seriously.
explain: 'It is (high) time' + past simple expresses that an action is overdue.

text: He talks as if he ___ everything already.
explain: 'As if' + past simple signals unreality or distance from fact.

text: No sooner ___ the door opened than the dog ran out.
explain: 'No sooner + had + subject + past participle + than...' — classic inversion.

text: She would rather her colleagues ___ credit her work openly.
explain: 'Would rather + subject + past simple' for present/future preferences about others.

text: ___ his experience, he couldn't solve the puzzle.
explain: 'For all' + noun phrase is a C1 concessive construction meaning 'despite'.

text: Were he ___ find out, there would be serious repercussions.
explain: Inverted hypothetical: 'Were + subject + to + infinitive' (= 'if he were to find out').

text: Little ___ that the decision would change everything.
explain: Fronted 'little' takes subject-auxiliary inversion for emphatic negation.

text: Not until the results were published ___ the truth.
explain: Fronted 'Not until + clause' triggers do-support inversion in the main clause.

text: Such ___ her reluctance that she refused to enter the room.
explain: Fronted 'Such + BE' inversion for emphasis: 'Such was X that…'.

text: He came across ___ arrogant, though he didn't mean to.
explain: 'Come across as + adjective' is the fixed collocation for making an impression.

text: The policy, ___ well-intended, proved disastrous in practice.
explain: 'Albeit' is a formal single-word conjunction meaning 'although'.

text: Scarcely ___ sat down when the fire alarm went off.
explain: Negative adverb 'Scarcely' at the front forces inversion.

text: The committee has yet ___ a final decision.
explain: 'Have yet to + infinitive' means something hasn't happened yet.

text: By next year, she ___ here for a full decade.
explain: Future perfect continuous expresses duration up to a future point.

text: Far be it ___ me to criticize, but the report is full of errors.
explain: 'Far be it from me' is a fossilized formulaic hedge before criticism.
